title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, caste, color, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement codeofconduct@posit.co. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/1/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired [Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder][https://github.com/mozilla/inclusion]. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: document outlines propose change scrutr. questions, please use discussions page rather opening issue.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: Small typos grammatical errors documentation may edited directly using GitHub web interface, long changes made source file. YES: edit roxygen comment dev/flat_*.Rmd file. : edit generated .Rd file man/, generated .R file R/.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: want make bigger change, open issue first discuss . Scope creep single biggest cause PRs rejected.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: Fork package clone . Create Git branch PR. scrutr uses fusen generate R/, man/, tests/testthat/ files dev/flat_*.Rmd. Edit relevant flat file, run fusen::inflate() regenerate. scrutr uses roxygen2, Markdown syntax, documentation. scrutr uses testthat. Contributions test cases easier accept. Make sure devtools::check() returns 0 errors / 0 warnings / 0 notes. Add bullet top NEWS.md, current development version header, describing user-visible change complete sentences. Open pull request.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: New code follow tidyverse style guide. Use styler apply styles, restyle code unrelated PR.

title: Contributing to scrutr
text: Please note scrutr project released Contributor Code Conduct. contributing project, agree abide terms.

title: Getting started with scrutr
code: library(scrutr)

title: Getting started with scrutr
text: inspect() produces one-row-per-variable summary: class, distinct count, missing values, void strings, character lengths, sample modalities. Use nrow = TRUE also print number observations:
code: result <- inspect(CO2) result #> # A tibble: 5 × 10 #>   variables class      nb_distinct prop_distinct nb_na prop_na nb_void prop_void #>   <chr>     <chr>            <int>         <dbl> <int>   <dbl>   <int>     <dbl> #> 1 Plant     ordered/f…          12        0.143      0       0       0         0 #> 2 Type      factor               2        0.0238     0       0       0         0 #> 3 Treatment factor               2        0.0238     0       0       0         0 #> 4 conc      numeric              7        0.0833     0       0       0         0 #> 5 uptake    numeric             76        0.905      0       0       0         0 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: nchars <chr>, modalities <chr> result <- inspect(CO2, nrow = TRUE) #> Number of observations: 84

title: Getting started with scrutr
text: working several related tables, often need know variables appear whether types consistent. vars_compclasses() goes compares class shared variable:
code: data_list <- list(   cars = cars,   mtcars = mtcars[, c("mpg", "hp", "wt", "speed")  |> intersect(names(mtcars))],   iris = iris )  # Which variables are in which datasets? vars_detect(data_list) #> # A tibble: 10 × 4 #>    vars_union   cars  mtcars iris  #>    <chr>        <chr> <chr>  <chr> #>  1 speed        ok    -      -     #>  2 dist         ok    -      -     #>  3 mpg          -     ok     -     #>  4 hp           -     ok     -     #>  5 wt           -     ok     -     #>  6 Sepal.Length -     -      ok    #>  7 Sepal.Width  -     -      ok    #>  8 Petal.Length -     -      ok    #>  9 Petal.Width  -     -      ok    #> 10 Species      -     -      ok # Use two datasets that share some columns shared_list <- list(   df1 = data.frame(x = 1:3, y = c("a", "b", "c"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE),   df2 = data.frame(x = c(1.1, 2.2, 3.3), y = c("d", "e", "f"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE) )  vars_compclasses(shared_list) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   vars_union df1       df2       #>   <chr>      <chr>     <chr>     #> 1 x          integer   numeric   #> 2 y          character character

what: Inspecting a whole folder of datasets
title: Getting started with scrutr
text: inspect_vars() main collection-level function. Point folder, reads matching files, inspects one, writes comprehensive Excel report.
code: # Create a temporary folder with example datasets mydir <- file.path(tempdir(), "scrutr_demo") dir.create(mydir, showWarnings = FALSE)  saveRDS(cars, file.path(mydir, "cars.rds")) saveRDS(mtcars, file.path(mydir, "mtcars.rds")) saveRDS(iris, file.path(mydir, "iris.rds"))  # Run the full inspection pipeline inspect_vars(   input_path = mydir,   output_path = mydir,   output_label = "demo",   considered_extensions = "rds" )  # The output Excel file contains multiple sheets: # dims, inspect_tot, one sheet per dataset, vars_detect, vars_compclasses, etc. list.files(mydir, pattern = "\\.xlsx$")

title: Getting started with scrutr
text: Convert matching files folder another format:
code: convert_all(   input_folderpath = mydir,   considered_extensions = "rds",   to = "csv",   output_folderpath = file.path(mydir, "csv") )

title: Getting started with scrutr
text: control, use Excel mask specifies exactly files convert name outputs:
code: # 1. Generate a mask template mask_convert_r(output_path = mydir)  # 2. Edit the mask in Excel, then: convert_r(   mask_filepath = file.path(mydir, "mask_convert_r.xlsx"),   output_path = mydir )

title: Getting started with scrutr
text: scrutr includes several utilities common data quality checks:
code: # Find duplicates in a data frame df <- data.frame(id = c(1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3), value = letters[1:6]) dupl_show(df, "id") #>   id value #> 1  2     b #> 2  2     c #> 3  3     d #> 4  3     e #> 5  3     f # Check a left join for key issues left_df <- data.frame(key = c("a", "b", "c")) right_df <- data.frame(key = c("a", "b", "b", "d"), val = 1:4) ljoin_checks(left_df, right_df, "key") #> Checks :  #> ltable rows : 3 #> rtable rows :4 #> jtable rows : 4 #> key are common var names accross the two tables #>   key val #> 1   a   1 #> 2   b   2 #> 3   b   3 #> 4   c  NA

title: Getting started with scrutr
code: paths <- c("data/raw/2024/file1.csv", "data/raw/2024/file2.csv")  # Keep only the first 2 levels path_move(paths, "/", 2) #> [1] "data/raw" "data/raw"  # Remove the last level (filename) path_move(paths, "/", -1) #> [1] "data/raw/2024" "data/raw/2024"

title: Authors and Citation
text: Daniel Rakotomalala. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Rakotomalala D (2026). scrutr: Scrutinizing Collections Structured Datasets. R package version 0.3.0.9000, https://github.com/danielrak/scrutr.
code: @Manual{,   title = {scrutr: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets},   author = {Daniel Rakotomalala},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.3.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/danielrak/scrutr}, }

title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
text: scrutr previously developed name industtry (2024-2025) renamed ahead first CRAN release. scrutr R toolkit scrutinizing collections structured datasets (data frames). targets workflows need apply inspection, profiling, conversion procedure across many related tables. Two guiding ideas: “Collections first”: common tasks (inspecting, detecting schema differences, batch conversion) operate collection level, one dataset time. “Operational tooling”: lightweight helpers paths, duplicates, join checks, string replacements recur production data pipelines.

title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("danielrak/scrutr")

title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
code: library(scrutr) result <- inspect(CO2) head(result)

what: Inspect a folder of datasets and export to Excel
title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
code: mydir <- file.path(tempdir(), "example") dir.create(mydir, showWarnings = FALSE)  saveRDS(cars, file.path(mydir, "cars.rds")) saveRDS(mtcars, file.path(mydir, "mtcars.rds"))  inspect_vars(   input_path = mydir,   output_path = mydir,   output_label = "diagnostics",   considered_extensions = "rds" )

title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
code: convert_all(   input_folderpath = mydir,   considered_extensions = "rds",   to = "csv",   output_folderpath = file.path(mydir, "csv_output") )

title: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets
code: # 1. Generate an Excel mask template mask_convert_r(output_path = mydir)  # 2. Fill in the mask, then run: convert_r(   mask_filepath = file.path(mydir, "mask_convert_r.xlsx"),   output_path = mydir )

what: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
title: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
text: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns symbol character vector

title: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
code: chars_structure(input_vector, unique = TRUE, named_output = TRUE)

title: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
text: input_vector Character. Vector process unique Logical 1L. TRUE, result reduced unique values. named_output Logical 1L. TRUE, output vector named corresponding input values.

title: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
text: Character. Vector describing structure element input_vector, see example.

title: Get :alpha: / :digit: patterns from each symbol of character vector — chars_structure
code: library(magrittr) input <- c("ABC123", "DE4F56", "789GHI", "ABC123")  # Default values of unique and named_output:  chars_structure(input_vector = input, unique = TRUE, named_output = TRUE) #>           ABC123           DE4F56           789GHI  #>         "3A, 3D" "2A, 1D, 1A, 2D"         "3D, 3A"   # unique is set to default value TRUE and named_output is set to FALSE:  chars_structure(input_vector = input, unique = TRUE, named_output = FALSE) #> [1] "3A, 3D"         "2A, 1D, 1A, 2D" "3D, 3A"          # unique is set to FALSE and named_output to FALSE:  chars_structure(input_vector = input, unique = FALSE, named_output = FALSE) #> [1] "3A, 3D"         "2A, 1D, 1A, 2D" "3D, 3A"         "3A, 3D"          # unique is set to FALSE and named_output to defalut value TRUE:  chars_structure(input_vector = input, unique = FALSE, named_output = TRUE) #>           ABC123           DE4F56           789GHI           ABC123  #>         "3A, 3D" "2A, 1D, 1A, 2D"         "3D, 3A"         "3A, 3D"

title: Generalized characters structure — chars_structure_general
text: Get user-defined patterns symbol character vector

title: Generalized characters structure — chars_structure_general
code: chars_structure_general(   input_vector,   split = "",   patterns_and_replacements,   unique = TRUE,   named_output = TRUE )

title: Generalized characters structure — chars_structure_general
text: input_vector Character. Vector process split Character 1L. Symbol separator patterns_and_replacements Character. Named character vector patterns (names) replacements (values) unique Logical 1L. TRUE, result reduced unique values named_output Logical 1L. TRUE, output vector named corresponding input values.

title: Generalized characters structure — chars_structure_general
text: character vector describing generalized structure element input_vector. named_output TRUE, vector named corresponding input values. unique TRUE, structures unique input values returned; otherwise one structure per element input_vector.

title: Generalized characters structure — chars_structure_general
code: input <- c("ABC123", "DE4F56", "789GHI", "ABC123")  # Default values of unique and named_output: chars_structure_general(input_vector = input, split = "",                          patterns_and_replacements = c("[:alpha:]" = "[letter]",                                                        "[:digit:]" = "[number]"),                          unique = TRUE, named_output = TRUE) #>                                       ABC123  #>                       "3[letter], 3[number]"  #>                                       DE4F56  #> "2[letter], 1[number], 1[letter], 2[number]"  #>                                       789GHI  #>                       "3[number], 3[letter]"   # unique is set to default value TRUE and named_output is set to FALSE: chars_structure_general(input_vector = input, split = "",                          patterns_and_replacements = c("[:alpha:]" = "[letter]",                                                        "[:digit:]" = "[number]"),                          unique = TRUE, named_output = FALSE) #> [1] "3[letter], 3[number]"                       #> [2] "2[letter], 1[number], 1[letter], 2[number]" #> [3] "3[number], 3[letter]"                        # unique is set to FALSE and named_output to FALSE: chars_structure_general(input_vector = input, split = "",                          patterns_and_replacements = c("[:alpha:]" = "[letter]",                                                        "[:digit:]" = "[number]"),                          unique = FALSE, named_output = FALSE) #> [1] "3[letter], 3[number]"                       #> [2] "2[letter], 1[number], 1[letter], 2[number]" #> [3] "3[number], 3[letter]"                       #> [4] "3[letter], 3[number]"                        # unique is set to FALSE and named_output to defalut value TRUE: chars_structure_general(input_vector = input, split = "",                          patterns_and_replacements = c("[:alpha:]" = "[letter]",                                                        "[:digit:]" = "[number]"),                          unique = FALSE, named_output = TRUE) #>                                       ABC123  #>                       "3[letter], 3[number]"  #>                                       DE4F56  #> "2[letter], 1[number], 1[letter], 2[number]"  #>                                       789GHI  #>                       "3[number], 3[letter]"  #>                                       ABC123  #>                       "3[letter], 3[number]"

what: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
title: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
text: Scan folder files matching specified extensions, convert one target format. Void character values replaced NA character whitespace trimmed.

title: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
code: convert_all(   input_folderpath,   considered_extensions,   to,   output_folderpath = input_folderpath )

title: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
text: input_folderpath Character. Folder path containing datasets convert. considered_extensions Character vector. File extensions include (must supported rio). Character. target output format (must supported rio). output_folderpath Character. Folder path converted files (defaults input_folderpath).

title: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
text: Invisibly returns character vector output file paths.

title: Convert all datasets within a folder to a given file format — convert_all
code: # \donttest{ mydir <- system.file("permadir_examples_and_tests/convert_all", package = "scrutr") outdir <- tempdir()  convert_all(input_folderpath = mydir,             considered_extensions = c("rds"),             to = "csv",             output_folderpath = outdir) #> Converting: original_cars.rds #>   Done: original_cars.csv  list.files(outdir, pattern = "csv$") #> [1] "original_cars.csv" # }

what: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
title: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
text: Read Excel mask describing files convert, import, clean, re-export file desired format. Cleaning includes replacing void character values ("") NA trimming whitespace.

title: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
code: convert_r(mask_filepath, output_path)

title: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
text: mask_filepath Character. Full file path Excel mask. mask must contain columns: folder_path, file, converted_file, to_convert (1 convert, 0 skip). output_path Character. Folder path converted files placed.

title: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
text: Invisibly returns character vector output file paths.

title: Batch dataset format conversion using an Excel mask — convert_r
code: mydir <- system.file("permadir_examples_and_tests/convert_r", package = "scrutr")  mask <- data.frame(   folder_path = rep(mydir, 2),   file = c("original_cars.rds", "original_mtcars.csv"),   converted_file = c("converted_cars.csv", "converted_mtcars.csv"),   to_convert = rep(1, 2) )  # \donttest{ mask_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "mask_convert_r.xlsx") writexl::write_xlsx(mask, mask_path)  convert_r(   mask_filepath = mask_path,   output_path = tempdir() ) #> Converting: original_cars.rds #>   Done: converted_cars.csv #> Converting: original_mtcars.csv #>   Done: converted_mtcars.csv  # Clean up: file.remove(file.path(tempdir(),   c("converted_cars.csv", "converted_mtcars.csv", "mask_convert_r.xlsx"))) #> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE # }

code: detect_chars_structure(vector, patterns, verbose = FALSE)

title: Detect character structure Detect if values within a character variable match at least one of defined patterns — detect_chars_structure
text: vector Character. Input vector detect pattern patterns Character. Patterns detect within vector. Regex supported verbose Logical 1L. TRUE, additional details related pattern detection provided

title: Detect character structure Detect if values within a character variable match at least one of defined patterns — detect_chars_structure
text: Logical 1L. verbose set TRUE, function returns list following elements order: "defined structure" : Logical 1L. TRUE pattern detected anywhere input vector "" : Character. Unique values input vector matching defined patterns "" : Integer. Indexes values input vector matching defined patterns

title: Detect character structure Detect if values within a character variable match at least one of defined patterns — detect_chars_structure
code: detect_chars_structure(   vector = c("ABCD1234", "4567EF", "89GHIJ10"),    patterns = "[:alpha:]{4}" # detect four consecutive alphabetic values ) #> [1] TRUE  detect_chars_structure(   vector = c("ABCD1234", "4567EF", "89GHIJ10"),    patterns = "[:alpha:]{4}",    verbose = TRUE ) #> $`Any defined structure` #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $Which #> [1] "ABCD1234" "89GHIJ10" #>  #> $Where #> [1] 1 3 #>

what: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
title: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
text: Detect character structure datasets

title: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
code: detect_chars_structure_datasets(   datasets_folderpath,   considered_extensions,   patterns,   output_filepath = file.path(datasets_folderpath, paste0("detect_chars_structure_",     basename(datasets_folderpath), ".rds")),   get_output_in_session = TRUE )

title: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
text: datasets_folderpath Character 1L. Folder path datasets process. datasets must root path considered_extensions Character. Datasets file extensions consider. Extensions must one supported rio:: package patterns Character. Patterns detect across datasets variables. Regex supported output_filepath Character 1L. Output folder path. get_output_in_session Logical 1L. TRUE, function return list, element element corresponds pattern detection details considered dataset

title: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
text: get_output_in_session TRUE, named list data frames (one per dataset file), columns var (variable name), any_defined_structure (logical), examples (character). list also saved RDS file output_filepath. get_output_in_session FALSE, function returns NULL invisibly called side effect writing RDS file.

title: Detect character structure from datasets — detect_chars_structure_datasets
code: mydir <- system.file("detect_chars_structure_datasets", package = "scrutr")  detect <- detect_chars_structure_datasets(   datasets_folderpath = mydir,    considered_extensions = "xlsx",    patterns = "(?i)college",    output_filepath = file.path(mydir, "detect_college.rds"),    get_output_in_session = TRUE)  # head(lapply(detect, head))  file.exists(file.path(mydir, "detect_college.rds")) #> [1] TRUE

what: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
title: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
text: Show observations duplicated values variable combination variables

title: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
code: dupl_show(data_frame, vars)

title: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
text: data_frame Data.frame. Input data frame. Must Global Environment data.frame class vars Character. Vector variable combination variables duplicates checked

title: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
text: Data.frame. part inputted data frame observations duplicated values indicated variable combination variables

title: Show observations of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_show
code: # A fictional data with duplicated values: df <- data.frame("person_id" = c(1, 1, 2, 3,                                  2, 4, 5, 5 ,1),                  "person_age" = c(25, 25, 21, 32,                                   21, 48, 50, 50, 52),                  "survey_month" = c("jan", "feb", "mar", "apr",                                     "apr", "may", "jun", "jul", "jan"),                  "survey_answer" = c("no", "yes", "no", "yes",                                      "yes", "yes", "no", "yes", NA))  # Shuffling observations and columns to make duplicates difficult to see: set.seed(1) df <- df[sample(1:nrow(df)),          sample(1:ncol(df))] df #>   person_id survey_month survey_answer person_age #> 7         5          jun            no         50 #> 3         2          mar            no         21 #> 6         4          may           yes         48 #> 2         1          feb           yes         25 #> 8         5          jul           yes         50 #> 5         2          apr           yes         21 #> 1         1          jan            no         25 #> 4         3          apr           yes         32 #> 9         1          jan          <NA>         52  # See all of the rows where person_id has more than an unique possible value:  dupl_show(data = df, var = "person_id") #>   person_id survey_month survey_answer person_age #> 1         1          feb           yes         25 #> 2         1          jan            no         25 #> 3         1          jan          <NA>         52 #> 4         2          mar            no         21 #> 5         2          apr           yes         21 #> 6         5          jun            no         50 #> 7         5          jul           yes         50  # See all of the rows where the combination of person_id and survey_month variables has  # more than an unique possible value :  dupl_show(data = df, var = c("person_id", "survey_month")) #>   person_id survey_month survey_answer person_age #> 1         1          jan            no         25 #> 2         1          jan          <NA>         52

title: Illustrate sources of all duplicated values of a variable or a combination of variables — dupl_sources
code: # A fictional data with duplicated values: df <- data.frame("person_id" = c(1, 1, 2, 3,                                  2, 4, 5, 5 ,1),                  "person_age" = c(25, 25, 21, 32,                                   21, 48, 50, 50, 52),                  "survey_month" = c("jan", "feb", "mar", "apr",                                     "apr", "may", "jun", "jul", "jan"),                  "survey_answer" = c("no", "yes", "no", "yes",                                      "yes", "yes", "no", "yes", NA))  # Shuffling observations and columns to make duplicates difficult to see: set.seed(1) df <- df[sample(1:nrow(df)),          sample(1:ncol(df))] df #>   person_id survey_month survey_answer person_age #> 7         5          jun            no         50 #> 3         2          mar            no         21 #> 6         4          may           yes         48 #> 2         1          feb           yes         25 #> 8         5          jul           yes         50 #> 5         2          apr           yes         21 #> 1         1          jan            no         25 #> 4         3          apr           yes         32 #> 9         1          jan          <NA>         52  dupl_sources(data_frame = df, vars = "person_id") #> $`Duplicate sources where person_id equals 1` #>                      values #> person_id                 1 #> survey_month  feb [AND] jan #> survey_answer  yes [AND] no #> person_age      25 [AND] 52 #>  #> $`Duplicate sources where person_id equals 2` #>                      values #> person_id                 2 #> survey_month  mar [AND] apr #> survey_answer  no [AND] yes #>  #> $`Duplicate sources where person_id equals 5` #>                      values #> person_id                 5 #> survey_month  jun [AND] jul #> survey_answer  no [AND] yes #>  dupl_sources(data_frame = df, vars = "person_id", output_as_df = TRUE) #>   person_id  survey_month survey_answer  person_age #> 1         1 feb [AND] jan  yes [AND] no 25 [AND] 52 #> 2         2 mar [AND] apr  no [AND] yes        <NA> #> 3         5 jun [AND] jul  no [AND] yes        <NA>

what: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
title: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
text: Replicate folder structure given directory

title: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
code: folder_structure_replicate(dir, to)

title: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
text: dir Character 1L. Path directory structure replicated Character 1L. Path output directory replicated structured placed

title: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
text: return value, called side effects. directory structure dir recreated inside directory.

title: Replicate the folder structure of a given directory — folder_structure_replicate
code: library(magrittr)  temp_dir_to_replicate <- tempfile() dir.create(temp_dir_to_replicate)  dir.create(file.path(temp_dir_to_replicate, "dir1")) dir.create(file.path(temp_dir_to_replicate, "dir2"))  temp_dir_out <- tempfile() dir.create(temp_dir_out)  folder_structure_replicate(   dir = temp_dir_to_replicate,    to = temp_dir_out)  unlink(temp_dir_to_replicate) unlink(temp_dir_out)

title: Inspect a data frame — inspect
text: Compute summary variable data frame: class, distinct values, missing values, void strings, character lengths, sample modalities.

title: Inspect a data frame — inspect
code: inspect(data_frame, nrow = FALSE)

title: Inspect a data frame — inspect
text: data_frame data frame inspect. nrow Logical. TRUE, number observations printed message addition returned table.

title: Inspect a data frame — inspect
text: tibble one row per variable columns: variables, class, nb_distinct, prop_distinct, nb_na, prop_na, nb_void, prop_void, nchars, modalities.

title: Inspect a data frame — inspect
code: inspect(CO2) #> # A tibble: 5 × 10 #>   variables class      nb_distinct prop_distinct nb_na prop_na nb_void prop_void #>   <chr>     <chr>            <int>         <dbl> <int>   <dbl>   <int>     <dbl> #> 1 Plant     ordered/f…          12        0.143      0       0       0         0 #> 2 Type      factor               2        0.0238     0       0       0         0 #> 3 Treatment factor               2        0.0238     0       0       0         0 #> 4 conc      numeric              7        0.0833     0       0       0         0 #> 5 uptake    numeric             76        0.905      0       0       0         0 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: nchars <chr>, modalities <chr>

what: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars
title: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars
text: Read datasets folder matching specified extensions, inspect one, write comprehensive Excel report covering variable presence, types, per-dataset summaries.

title: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars
code: inspect_vars(   input_path,   output_path,   output_label,   considered_extensions,   encoding = NULL )

title: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars
text: input_path Character. Folder path containing datasets explore. output_path Character. Folder path Excel output written. output_label Character. concise label output file name. considered_extensions Character vector. File extensions include (without dot, e.g. "rds" ".rds"). encoding Character 1L NULL. Encoding passed rio::import() text-based formats (CSV, TSV). Must one "unknown", "UTF-8", "Latin-1". Defaults NULL (let rio / data.table pick default). Ignored binary formats (e.g. RDS, xlsx).

title: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars
text: Invisibly returns path written Excel file. file contains sheets: dims, inspect_tot, one per dataset inspection, vars_detect, vars_detect_everywhere, vars_detect_not_everywhere, vars_compclasses, vars_compclasses_allsame, vars_compclasses_not_allsame.

title: Inspect a collection of datasets — inspect_vars

what: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
title: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
text: Inspect data frame write output Excel file

title: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
code: inspect_write(data_frame, output_path, output_label = NULL)

title: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
text: data_frame data frame inspect. output_path Character. Folder path Excel output written. output_label Character. Optional label output file. NULL, defaults deparsed name data_frame.

title: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
text: Invisibly returns path written Excel file. file contains inspection table prepended observation variable counts.

title: Inspect a data frame and write the output to an Excel file — inspect_write
code: mydir <- file.path(tempdir(), "inspect_write_example") dir.create(mydir) inspect_write(data_frame = cars,               output_path = mydir,               output_label = "cars") readxl::read_xlsx(file.path(mydir, "inspect_cars.xlsx")) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   `1:nrow(i)` variables class   nb_distinct prop_distinct nb_na prop_na nb_void #>   <chr>       <chr>     <chr>   <chr>       <chr>         <chr> <chr>   <chr>   #> 1 Obs =       50        NA      NA          NA            NA    NA      NA      #> 2 Nvars =     2         NA      NA          NA            NA    NA      NA      #> 3 1           speed     numeric 19          0.38          0     0       0       #> 4 2           dist      numeric 35          0.7           0     0       0       #> # ℹ 3 more variables: prop_void <chr>, nchars <chr>, modalities <chr> unlink(mydir, recursive = TRUE)

what: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
title: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
text: Perform classical dplyr::left_join() add check information related join

title: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
code: ljoin_checks(ltable, rtable, ...)

title: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
text: ltable Data.frame. Left data frame join rtable Data.frame. Right data frame join ... arguments passed dplyr::left_join().

title: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
text: Data.frame. Output dplyr::left_join() messages number observations left, right joined data frames list common variables ltable rtable

title: Perform a classical dplyr::left_join() and add check information related to join — ljoin_checks
code: left_table <- data.frame("person_id" = c(1, 1, 2, 3,                                  2, 4, 5, 5 ,1),                  "person_age" = c(25, 25, 21, 32,                                   21, 48, 50, 50, 52),                  "survey_month" = c("jan", "feb", "mar", "apr",                                     "apr", "may", "jun", "jul", "jan"),                  "survey_answer" = c("no", "yes", "no", "yes",                                      "yes", "yes", "no", "yes", NA))  right_table <- data.frame("person_id" = c(2, 5, 4, 3, 1),                            "person_name" = c("John", "Marie", "Pierre", "Marc", "Jimmy"))  list("left_table" = left_table,       "right_table" = right_table) #> $left_table #>   person_id person_age survey_month survey_answer #> 1         1         25          jan            no #> 2         1         25          feb           yes #> 3         2         21          mar            no #> 4         3         32          apr           yes #> 5         2         21          apr           yes #> 6         4         48          may           yes #> 7         5         50          jun            no #> 8         5         50          jul           yes #> 9         1         52          jan          <NA> #>  #> $right_table #>   person_id person_name #> 1         2        John #> 2         5       Marie #> 3         4      Pierre #> 4         3        Marc #> 5         1       Jimmy #>   ljoin_checks(left_table, right_table, by = "person_id") #> Checks :  #> ltable rows : 9 #> rtable rows :5 #> jtable rows : 9 #> person_id are common var names accross the two tables #>   person_id person_age survey_month survey_answer person_name #> 1         1         25          jan            no       Jimmy #> 2         1         25          feb           yes       Jimmy #> 3         2         21          mar            no        John #> 4         3         32          apr           yes        Marc #> 5         2         21          apr           yes        John #> 6         4         48          may           yes      Pierre #> 7         5         50          jun            no       Marie #> 8         5         50          jul           yes       Marie #> 9         1         52          jan          <NA>       Jimmy

what: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
title: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
text: Create excel mask compatible convert_r() function

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
code: mask_convert_r(output_path, output_filename = "mask_convert_r.xlsx")

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
text: output_path Character 1L. Folder path mask created output_filename Character 1L. File name (extension) mask

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
text: return value, called side effects. Excel file (.xlsx) written file.path(output_path, output_filename) containing empty template columns folder_path, file, converted_file, to_convert.

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the convert_r() function — mask_convert_r
code: mydir <- file.path(tempdir(), "convert_r_tests_examples") dir.create(mydir)  mask_convert_r(output_path = mydir)  list.files(mydir) #> [1] "mask_convert_r.xlsx"  unlink(mydir, recursive = TRUE)

what: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
title: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
text: Create excel mask compatible rename_r() function. must used collection files, .e stored within folder.

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
code: mask_rename_r(input_path, output_filename = "mask_rename_r.xlsx")

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
text: input_path Character 1L. Folder containing set files rename output_filename Character 1L. File name excel mask.

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
text: return value, called side effects. Excel mask file written input_path.

title: Create an excel mask compatible with the rename_r() function. — mask_rename_r
code: library(magrittr) data(cars) data(mtcars)  mydir <- tempfile() dir.create(mydir)  saveRDS(cars, file.path(mydir, "cars.rds")) saveRDS(mtcars, file.path(mydir, "mtcars.rds"))  list.files(mydir) #> [1] "cars.rds"   "mtcars.rds"  mask_rename_r(input_path = mydir)  list.files(mydir) #> [1] "cars.rds"           "mask_rename_r.xlsx" "mtcars.rds"          readxl::read_xlsx(file.path(mydir, "mask_rename_r.xlsx")) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   file       renamed_file to_rename #>   <chr>      <lgl>        <lgl>     #> 1 cars.rds   NA           NA        #> 2 mtcars.rds NA           NA         unlink(mydir, recursive = TRUE)

title: Move through paths — path_move
text: Move paths

title: Move through paths — path_move
code: path_move(path_vector, path_separator = "/", move)

title: Move through paths — path_move
text: path_vector Character. Vector paths equal number levels. path_separator Character. Path separator (adapted OS, e.g. "/"). move Integer. positive, outputs path specified level. negative, removes last specified level(s).

title: Move through paths — path_move
text: Character vector transformed paths.

title: Move through paths — path_move
code: pvector <- c(   "level_1/level_2/level_3/file_1.ext",   "level_1/level_2/level_3/file_2.ext" )  path_move(path_vector = pvector,           path_separator = "/",           move = 1) #> [1] "level_1" "level_1"  path_move(path_vector = pvector,           path_separator = "/",           move = 2) #> [1] "level_1/level_2" "level_1/level_2"  path_move(path_vector = pvector,           path_separator = "/",           move = -1) #> [1] "level_1/level_2/level_3" "level_1/level_2/level_3"  path_move(path_vector = pvector,           path_separator = "/",           move = -2) #> [1] "level_1/level_2" "level_1/level_2"

title: Industrialized file renaming — rename_r
text: Industrialized file renaming

title: Industrialized file renaming — rename_r
code: rename_r(mask_filepath)

title: Industrialized file renaming — rename_r
text: mask_filepath Character 1L. Entire file path excel mask

title: Industrialized file renaming — rename_r
text: return value, called side effects. Files renamed disk according instructions Excel mask.

title: Industrialized file renaming — rename_r
code: library(magrittr) data(cars) data(mtcars)  mydir <- tempfile() dir.create(mydir)  # Two example files to rename:  saveRDS(cars, file.path(mydir, "cars.rds")) saveRDS(mtcars, file.path(mydir, "mtcars.rds")) list.files(mydir) #> [1] "cars.rds"   "mtcars.rds"  # Create the mask:  mask_rename_r(input_path = mydir)  # Fill the mask (in practice you can do it manually):  mask <- rio::import(file.path(mydir, "mask_rename_r.xlsx")) mask[["renamed_file"]] <- c("cars_renamed.rds", "mtcars_renamed.rds") mask[["to_rename"]] <- rep(1, 2) writexl::write_xlsx(mask, file.path(mydir, "mask_rename_r.xlsx"))  # Apply the rename function:  rename_r(mask_filepath = file.path(mydir, "mask_rename_r.xlsx")) #> Renamed: cars.rds -> cars_renamed.rds #> Renamed: mtcars.rds -> mtcars_renamed.rds  # See the renamed files:  list.files(mydir) #> [1] "cars_renamed.rds"   "mask_rename_r.xlsx" "mtcars_renamed.rds"  # Clean tempdir:  unlink(mydir, recursive = TRUE)

what: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
text: Names replacements matched literally (regular expressions). Elements input_vector match none patterns returned unchanged.

title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
code: replace_multiple(input_vector, replacements, replace_all = FALSE)

title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
text: input_vector Character. Character vector replacements take place. replacements Character. Named character vector defining replacement correspondences (names = patterns, values = replacements). Names treated literal strings (regex metacharacters ., (, + interpreted). replace_all Logical. TRUE, stringr::str_replace_all() used instead stringr::str_replace().

title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
text: Character vector replacements applied.

title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
text: several patterns can match element, first match found input_vector (scanned left right) used. patterns start position, one listed first replacements wins, longest. Order replacements accordingly patterns prefixes others.

title: Replace character vector values using a correspondence approach — replace_multiple
code: input <- c("one-one", "two-two-one", "three-three-two")  replace_multiple(input,                  replacements =                    c("one" = "1", "two" = "2",                      "three" = "3")) #> [1] "1-one"       "2-two-one"   "3-three-two"  replace_multiple(input,                  replacements =                    c("one" = "1", "two" = "2",                      "three" = "3"),                  replace_all = TRUE) #> [1] "1-1"     "2-2-one" "3-3-two"  # Unmatched elements are returned as-is: replace_multiple(c("one", "unmatched"), c("one" = "1")) #> [1] "1"         "unmatched"  # Regex metacharacters are matched literally: replace_multiple(c("a.b", "aXb"), c("." = "DOT")) #> [1] "aDOTb" "aXb"

what: scrutr: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets — scrutr-package
title: scrutr: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets — scrutr-package
text: Provides coherent interface exploring transforming multiple related data frames share common structure. Complements single-dataset inspection tools operating across entire collection . Also includes lightweight utilities related file folder management tasks.

title: scrutr: Scrutinizing Collections of Structured Datasets — scrutr-package
text: Maintainer: Daniel Rakotomalala rakdanielh@gmail.com

title: scrutr 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-22

title: scrutr 0.3.0
text: Package renamed industtry scrutr. Removed serial_import(), parallel_import(), assign_to_global(), current_script_location(). relied global environment side effects RStudio-dependencies. inspect_vars() now returns named list data frames instead assigning global environment. vars_detect(), vars_compclasses(), related functions now accept named list data frames instead character vectors object names. inspect_write() now takes data frame object directly (data_frame) instead string name (data_frame_name).

title: scrutr 0.3.0
text: Removed non-CRAN dependencies: job, plyr, rstudioapi. Eliminated global environment pollution (assign(), get(), <<-). convert_all() now synchronous (longer requires RStudio background jobs). inspect() collapses multi-element class vectors (e.g. ordered/factor) single string per variable. path_move() uses fixed = TRUE strsplit() correct separator handling. Modernized deprecated dplyr::mutate_if()/mutate_all() dplyr::across(). Comprehensive test suite rewritten new API.

title: scrutr 0.2.0
text: Enhancements short docs. Add long docs. Polish.

title: scrutr 0.1.0
text: Development enhancements: Add examples tests far possible. Correction warnings notes. complete documentation.
